Chapter VII: The Origins of the Apex Supremacy

In the quiet reclusion of the Workers Party of Korea's dominion, a technological titan arose from the most unexpected of places. As the world busied itself with Cold War rivalries and ideological battles across distant lands, it remained largely unaware of the digital revolution quietly unfolding within the Democratic People’s Republic of Korea. This turning point in history began not in California's heralded Silicon Valley, but in the securely guarded, enigmatic nation of North Korea, under the leadership of an American-born visionary named Joe Tae-se.

Joe, born in San Francisco to a Korean father and an American mother, embodied a cultural symbiosis from an early age. This bicultural heritage was to particular advantage when he founded the Apex Corporation in 1972 amidst the fanfare of Pyongyang's futuristic aspirations. Melding Eastern discipline with Western ingenuity, Apex rapidly ascended to the zenith of innovation, surpassing its global contemporaries. 

The advent of the Apex I personal computer in 1975 encapsulated Joe's dream of a connected world. Remarkably user-friendly and accessible, it soon became a household name within the Democratic People’s Republic. North Korea, despite its international isolation, was becoming an inconspicuous epicenter for technological advancement. 

By the 1980s, North Korea's metamorphosis had reached a tipping point. The capital inflow from Apex’s ubiquitous products facilitated a gradual but profound transition towards capitalist practices. Premier Lee Dong-won, a moderate by the country's standards, orchestrated a pivotal collaboration with Joe. This partnership aimed to capitalize on North Korea's untapped labor force for the subsidized manufacturing of Apex computers. The mutual benefit was unmistakable—Pyongyang fueled its economic engine, while Apex enjoyed a competitive advantage unrivaled across the globe. 

The year 1985 marked the creation of the World Wide Web, not by European physicists, but by Apex technicians within Pyongyang's state-of-the-art facilities. With the digital epoch in full swing, Apex's revenues swelled to an extraordinary $101 billion by 1988. The company's influence penetrated every stratum of society and altered the geopolitical terrain forever.

Unbeknownst to the public, the United States government, embroiled in its own economic challenges and hobbling technological industry, turned to Apex for salvation. Unwilling to concede their waning dominance, American officials orchestrated a covert operation to maintain at least a façade of technological preeminence. Steve Jobs and Bill Gates emerged as the poster boys for America's innovation, founding Apple and Microsoft with Apex’s clandestine assistance. The charade was flawlessly staged, veiling the truth of a digital revolution born in the heart of communist Asia.

The global orthodoxy continued to perceive North Korea through a lens of poverty and desolation—dreamers of power but forever its mourners. Yet beneath this masquerade, a behemoth stirred. Apex and its founder Joe stood as architects of a world reimagined, their influence transcending borders and ideologies.

Japan's trajectory, in contrast, was one of continued ascension. Rejecting the complete dissolution of its feudal past, Japan melded the shogunate's structure with modern governance following the Meiji Restoration of 1868. Its defeat in the protracted Pacific War did little to staunch the societal and economic burgeoning that followed. Amid the shadow of post-war reconstruction, Japan's rekindled imperial and aristocratic elements forged an economic miracle.

As the 21st century dawned, Japan emerged not just as a regional powerhouse, but as a sanctum of economic and cultural dominance. The Western world's myopic focus on containment and Russian affairs left Japan’s influence unchecked. The Japanese managed a harmonious reconciliation of political factions in Taiwan, devising what would be lauded as a masterstroke of diplomacy—the One Country, Two Systems protocol. Thus, began the meteoric rise of the Greater Japanese State, casting its ever-expanding shadow across East Asia and eventually the very world.

Under the astute premiership of Shinzo Abe, Japan advanced the integration of state-of-the-art technology and military might. This was not restricted to domestic prowess but extended its tendrils through a state-controlled Apex Corporation. The tentacles of the Japanese empire, once content to linger in the Pacific, now reached defiantly towards distant horizons, encircling nations with their blend of technological splendor and veiled imperialism. 

When Korea—one peninsula undivided but split in governance—was finally ensnared, the result was a swift annexation into the ever-growing sphere of Nippon. Chōsen and Formosa served as cornerstones to Japan's new world order, their militaries and technologies absorbed into the titanic being that was the Self-Defense Forces. Arrayed with a multi-million-strong fighting force and an arsenal assimilated from the greatest powers of the age, Japan now presided over an empire whose influence echoed those of ancient times.

Though controversies and conflicts wrought by Japanese expansionism rippled across geopolitical waters, none proved capable of stemming the tide. The exertions to overt Operations Water Snake and the formation of the Pacific States of America bespoke a new reality. Resistance may linger, but with each passing month, the Japanese hegemony solidified its foundations while elsewhere, once-mighty empires crumbled.

This is the world of the Apex supremacy: an age shadowed by the empire of the Rising Sun, an electronic panopticon at its zenith, and a revolution that, once kindled in the seemingly most improbable of hearts, shifted the course of history irreversibly.

Chapter VIII: The Silent Convergence

Amid the teeming crowds of Taipei, the tapestry of modernity was being interwoven with the ancient threads of empire. It was done quietly, a complex game of shadows that pitted the precision of the Japanese Self-Defense Forces (JSDF) against Taiwanese factions clinging to the vestiges of nationalism. The world beyond the island’s vibrant shores remained blissfully ignorant of the stratagems at play.

Beneath the white-hot glare of neon signs, the Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office (CIRO) operatives worked their silent craft. The glow of Taipei's skyline masked the meticulous machinations that pierced the night with whispered codes and clandestine meetings. The Japanese government's promise of peace between the Taiwanese and the Nationalist Chinese remnants was but a veneer for the surgical precision with which the Special Forces Group (SFG) neutralized dissidents.

Shinzo Abe, the orchestrator of the island's subjugation under the 'One country, two systems' doctrine, had done so with a Nobel Peace Prize in his pocket. The prize was a tribute to the public façade, even as the underbelly of his operation saw the ruthless dispatch of those who dared oppose the silent convergence. The SFG, shadows within shadows, played a vital role—trained to the pinnacle of military perfection with an arsenal derived from global technological supremacy.

In the cobwebbed back-alleys of backdoor negotiations, the Public Security Intelligence Agency (PSIA) extended its reach into the hearts of Taiwanese opposition. They struck with precision, crippling the infrastructure of resistance before it could spring to life. The whispers on the street spoke of treachery within, but the PSIA made sure those dissenting voices found neither audience nor echo.

"It's only a matter of time," murmured Commander Saito to his team of SAT operatives as they gathered in the safe house layered in the shadow of the Taipei 101.

Their target was a coalition of resistance fighters, a group of nationalist soldiers unwilling to let go of a free Taiwan. The group had been planning a desperate strike against the JSDF and their integration forces, aiming to galvanize international scrutiny and intervention. They were led by General Huang, a man of fierce conviction and unyielding loyalty to the old guard of the Republic of China.

But Huang didn’t account for the CIRO mole embedded in his inner circle—Nor for the electronic surveillance that turned his every plan into an open book for Saito and his adept squadron.

In contrast, the situation with American and Russian military personnel proved to be a chess game of higher stakes. Trade deals and offers of shared technology ensured a smooth transition of power, with only a few defectors who saw through the charade. Quiet deals within the Kremlin and veiled threats of economic consequences kept Russian dissenters in check. As for the Americans, the double-edged sword of debt and diplomatic pressure allowed the Japanese transition to occur without a semblance of external military intervention.

"We have to hit hard and fast. Neutralize the threat before it blooms into chaos," Commander Saito stated as he activated his matte black cybernetic visor, the feed showing the heartbeats of his quarry through concrete walls.

The SAT teams moved out, blending with the darkness as the city blazed on oblivious above. They infiltrated Huang's compound, a decrepit office building marked for demolition but repurposed for the cause of freedom. The night erupted with the reverberating hiss of silenced weaponry and the soft thuds of incapacitated bodies crumpling to the ground. In less than an hour, the SAT had apprehended Huang and his cohorts without a single victim or shot fired in the urban sprawl that stood witness to their efficacy.

In the following days, the international community remained none the wiser. Newsfeeds filled not with tales of abduction or political suppression but of economic rejuvenation, technological partnership, and the bright future of a united Asia under Japanese guidance. The meta-narrative was controlled, and the populace ate it up with fervor.

Meanwhile, the remnants of the KMT and obstinate nationalists found themselves ferried away to black sites, where their loyalty to the old order was to be forgotten. The masterful influence of the CIRO and PSIA, coupled with the decisive action of the SFG and SAT, had ensured a seamless transition.

The reunification of Taiwan and Japan was a fait accompli—the Silent Convergence was now but a chapter in history, buried under layers of propaganda and cyber-manipulated truths. The Great Japanese State had expanded once more, with a peace so carefully manufactured and a silence so deep it resonated across the tides of time.

Chapter IX: The Re-ascension Protocol

On the steel-grey decks of the JSDF's Izumo-class destroyer 'Kaga', the world seemed motionless, absent the din of geopolitical turbulence. Yet beneath the stoic exterior, a revolution churned. Prime Minister Shinzo Abe's decree had long been unveiled – a new era of militaristic renovation was unfolding as a silhouetted sun rose over the once-independent island of Formosa.

"Out with the obsolete; in with the unassailable," declared General Kurosawa, overseeing the demolition of antiquated tanks and artillery. The JSDF was shedding the skin of eras past. Technology from the recently inculcated Republic of China Armed Forces—now relics of a bygone age—fueled the forges of Japan's burgeoning might. The Airborne Special Service Company, Taiwan's elite squad, was to be integrated into the Special Forces Group, their initiation trials rigorous and unrelenting to forge warriors of equal caliber.

The National Security Bureau, once the clandestine spear of Taiwanese espionage, found themselves amidst a merger of both purpose and identity within the CIRO and PSIA. Assets were evaluated, repurposed, or eradicated in a sweeping intelligence reorganization that absorbed decades of subterfuge into the comprehensive web spun by Japanese security systems.

Emperor Akihito, embodying the serenity of his reign, endorsed the colossal undertaking with ceremonial grace, a gentle hand guiding the cascading transformation.
